title: Magnetic self-frustration from spontaneous structural distortion
authors: T. Vignau Costa, G. L. Rossini, D. C. Cabra, S. A. Grigera, R. A. Borzi
abstract: In frustrated magnetism, lattice distortions mediated by magnetoelastic coupling are commonly invoked as an escape route from extensive degeneracy toward an ordered ground state and, in some cases, the onset of multiferroicity. Here we present a minimal classical model that illustrates the converse phenomenon, which we term ``magnetic self-frustration''. Monte Carlo simulations reveal that a kagomé lattice with trivial magnetic interactions -- namely, nearest-neighbor Ising ferromagnetism -- undergoes a magnetostructural transition into a breathing-like phase, characterized by irregular bond dimerization along the three kagomé directions. Structurally, the equilateral triangles belonging to one of the two kagomé sublattices spontaneously distort, expanding into isosceles triangles. An analysis to first order in the magnetoelastic coupling constant $α$ shows that the shape of these triangles is remarkably robust. Acting like rigid building blocks in a puzzle, their vertices determine the geometry of the second sublattice, giving rise to contracted ferromagnetic triangles with a variety of shapes. The magnetic sector can be mapped onto an effective antiferromagnetic triangular lattice, which remains disordered down to low temperatures and retains a finite residual entropy of one third of that of Wannier. This self-frustrated phase takes place at intermediate values of $α$, separating the conventional undistorted ferromagnetic phase at weak coupling from a strongly coupled ordered phase characterized by a regular dimerized up-up-down-down antiferromagnetic pattern along the three kagomé directions, built from ferromagnetic triangles and hexagons.
summary: This theoretical work models magnetic frustration arising from magnetoelastic coupling in a kagomé lattice. It shows that the system transitions through a self-frustrated phase, where structural distortions generate inherent magnetic frustration even when the initial magnetic interactions are simple. This mechanism provides a novel route to understanding complex magnetic ground states in correlated materials.
main_problem: The paper investigates 'magnetic self-frustration,' a phenomenon where magnetoelastic coupling induces magnetic frustration in an otherwise non-frustrated system.
main_result: The system undergoes a magnetostructural transition into a self-frustrated (SF) phase, characterized by spontaneous structural distortions and residual magnetic entropy, separating the simple ferromagnetic and highly ordered checkerboard phases.
method: The study employs classical Monte Carlo simulations and analytical linear approximations to model the coupled spin-lattice Hamiltonian.
model_or_system: The system is an Ising spin model on a kagomé lattice coupled to local elastic distortions via a magnetoelastic coupling constant $\alpha$. The Hamiltonian includes terms for magnetic exchange and elastic energy.
key_observables: Magnetization modulus ($|M|$), Breathing order parameter ($OP_{Breath}$), Specific heat ($C_V$), and residual entropy.
important_parameters: The magnetoelastic coupling constant $\alpha$ relative to a critical value $\alpha_c$, and temperature $T$.
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on expanding the exchange coupling to first order in the distance change and using harmonic oscillator models for site displacements.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the phase diagram ($\alpha-T$) showing FM, SF, and CB boundaries, and show histograms of magnetic exchange couplings demonstrating the robustness of certain AFM bonds in the SF phase.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by defining the Hamiltonian, simulating the system across different $\alpha$ regimes (FM $\to$ SF $\to$ CB), and analyzing the resulting structural and magnetic order parameters to map out the phase diagram.
why_interesting: The concept of dynamically generated frustration via lattice coupling is highly relevant to understanding emergent quantum phases in strongly correlated materials, particularly those exhibiting structural instabilities.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. a- A fragment of the kagom´e lattice. A distortion at site i is indicated together with its effect on the neighboring exchange couplings (Jij = 1 −α (rij −1), see Eq. (2)). Bond- shortening favors ferromagnetism, while enough lengthening leads to antiferromagnetism. b- The checkerboard configura- tion on the kagom´e lattice, stable at large α values. Arrows in- dicate ion displacements from the pristine lattice (gray dots). The distorted configuration (blue dots) consists of contracted triangles and hexagons; along the three kagom´e directions there is a length dimerization, seen as an alternation of short (ferro) and long (antiferro) bonds. Spins are aligned ferromag- netically in...

FIG. 2. a) The phase diagram α −T for the elastic kagom´e lattice. The color plot is an interpolation of the specific heat data from from the Monte Carlo curves. We use the CV peaks to trace the phase boundaries (black points; the white dashed lines are guides to the eye) between the paramagnetic phase (PM), ferromagnetic (FM), Checkerboard (CB), and self-frustrated (SF). The frontier between the SF and the CB phase is an exception, with white points taken from the max- imum of the order parameter fluctuations for the SF phase. Yellow dashed lines mark the steep low temperature bound- aries deduced from the measurement of entropies and energies at very low temperature. The vertical lines...

FIG. 4. Entropy per spin vs. temperature for representative values of the magnetoelastic coupling α/αc = 0.56, 1.3, 1.86 (using the same color code as in Fig. 3). The residual entropy (1/3 of the value of the residual entropy found by Wannier for the antiferromagnetic triangular lattice) for intermediate α evidences that in this case the order is partial.

FIG. 3. Panel a): Specific heat vs. temperature for three rep- resentative values of the magnetoelastic coupling α/αc (see caption). The peak in CV in each curve marks a phase tran- sition into different ordered states, characterized by suitable order parameters. Panels b) to d): Order parameters as a function of temperature. Each parameter characterizes a dis- tinct type of order appearing in different ranges of the mag- netoelastic coupling: the magnetization modulus |M| for the FM phase at low α/αc (red data), the breathing parameter for the SF phase at intermediate α/αc (green data), and the CB order parameter for large α/αc (orange data).

FIG. 5. A microstate within the degenerate SF phase ground state. The spontaneous symmetry breaking is reflected in that all “up” triangles (green) are stretched to antiferromagnetic exchanges (AFTs), while all “down” triangles (light red and blue according to their spin orientation) are compressed rein- forcing ferromagnetic couplings (FMTs). AFTs have isosceles distortions, with their symmetry axis lying along one of three possible directions. Their shape is robust: two particular val- ues of Jij > 0 repeat along the lattice for all AFTs (thick and thin green lines), in all microstates.
